content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 27 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 113v–113r | **Source Images:** DEU_027_page_001.jpg, DEU_027_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 159 | **Images:** DEU_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Now Moses and the elders of Israel commanded the people, saying, "Keep all the commandment that I command you today.

**2** And on the day you cross over the Jordan to the land that the LORD your God is giving you, you shall set up large stones and plaster them with plaster.

**3** And you shall write on them all the words of this instruction when you cross over, so that you may enter the land that the LORD your God is giving you, a land flowing with milk and honey, as the LORD, the God of your fathers, has promised you.

**4** And when you have crossed over the Jordan, you shall set up these stones, about which I am commanding you today, on Mount Ebal, and you shall plaster them with plaster.

**5** And you shall build there an altar to the LORD your God, an altar of stones. You shall wield no iron tool on them.

**6** You shall build the altar of the LORD your God of uncut stones, and you shall offer burnt offerings on it to the LORD your God,

**7** and you shall sacrifice peace offerings and shall eat there, and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God.

**8** And you shall write on the stones all the words of this instruction very plainly."

**9** Then Moses and the Levitical priests spoke to all Israel, saying, "Keep silence and hear, O Israel: this day you have become the people of the LORD your God.

**10** You shall therefore obey the voice of the LORD your God, keeping his commandments and his statutes, which I command you today."

**11** That day Moses commanded the people, saying,

**12** "When you have crossed over the Jordan, these shall stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.

**13** And these shall stand on Mount Ebal for the curse: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.

**14** And the Levites shall declare to all the men of Israel in a loud voice:

**15** "'Cursed be the man who makes a carved or cast metal image, an abomination to the LORD, a thing made by the hands of a craftsman, and sets it up in secret.' And all the people shall answer and say, 'Amen.'

**16** "'Cursed be the one who dishonors his father or his mother.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**17** "'Cursed be the one who moves his neighbor's landmark.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**18** "'Cursed be the one who misleads a blind person on the road.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**19** "'Cursed be the one who perverts the justice due to the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**20** "'Cursed be the one who lies with his father's wife, because he has uncovered his father's covering.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**21** "'Cursed be the one who lies with any kind of animal.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**22** "'Cursed be the one who lies with his sister, whether the daughter of his father or the daughter of his mother.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**23** "'Cursed be the one who lies with his mother-in-law.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**24** "'Cursed be the one who strikes down his neighbor in secret.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**25** "'Cursed be the one who takes a bribe to shed innocent blood.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'

**26** "'Cursed be the one who does not confirm the words of this instruction by doing them.' And all the people shall say, 'Amen.'"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 27**

**Plastered stones — avanei sid (vv. 2–4, 8):** The stones are to be plastered with *sid* (שִׂיד), lime plaster, and the Torah written on them. This practice is attested in the ancient Near East; the Mesha Stele and Egyptian boundary stelae provide archaeological parallels. The LXX has *koniate autous konian* (κονίατε αὐτοὺς κονίαν), "plaster them with plaster," preserving the redundant emphasis of the Hebrew.

**"Very plainly" (v. 8):** *ba'er hetev* — "explaining/making clear thoroughly." The doubled adverb (intensive *ba'er* + *hetev*, "well/thoroughly") emphasizes that the inscription must be legible and complete. The LXX has *saphes sphodra* (σαφῶς σφόδρα), "very clearly," equivalently intensive. Some medieval Jewish commentators (following Deut. Rab.) held that the Torah was written in all seventy languages of the nations at this moment.

**Mount Ebal vs. Mount Gerizim (vv. 12–13):** The tribes are divided across two mountains flanking Shechem in the central highlands. Gerizim (blessings) and Ebal (curses) represent the binary structure of covenant consequence. The Samaritan Pentateuch alters the text to place the altar and inscription on Gerizim rather than Ebal (v. 4), reflecting the Samaritan claim that Gerizim is the true sacred mountain. The Leningrad Codex (followed here) reads Ebal for v. 4; the LXX Vaticanus also reads *Gaibai* (= Ebal), supporting the Masoretic text against the Samaritan revision.

**Arur — "Cursed" (vv. 15–26):** The Hebrew *arur* (אָרוּר, passive participle of *arar*) is a declaratory curse formula. I render "Cursed be the one who..." throughout, preserving the passive/declared character. The LXX uses *epikataratos* (ἐπικατάρατος, "accursed") consistently, with *ho anthropos hos* ("the man who"), slightly more specific than the Hebrew *ish*. The twelve curses frame violations of secret sin — acts that might escape human courts but not divine sanction.

**"Uncut stones" — avanot shlemot (v. 6):** Literally "whole/complete stones," i.e., undressed. The altar must be built without iron tools (v. 5), echoing Exodus 20:25. The LXX has *lithous holoklerous* (λίθους ὁλοκλήρους, "whole stones"), exactly matching the Hebrew *shlemot* (from *shalem*, wholeness).

**"Confirms the words" — yaqim (v. 26):** The twelfth and summarizing curse falls on *kol asher lo yaqim et-divrei ha-torah ha-zot la'asot otam* — "everyone who does not uphold the words of this instruction to do them." The verb *qum* in the Hiphil (*yaqim*) means "to cause to stand, to establish, to uphold." The LXX has *emmenei* (ἐμμένει, "abides/remains"), a somewhat weaker rendering; the Hebrew emphasizes active, structural upholding of the entire law, not merely remaining within it. Paul quotes this verse in Galatians 3:10.

**"Dishonors his father or mother" (v. 16):** *maqeleh aviv ve-immo* — literally "the one who makes light of/treats lightly his father and his mother." The verb *qalal* (make light/treat with contempt) stands in contrast to the command to *kabed* (honor/make heavy) one's parents (Exod 20:12). The LXX has *atimason* (ἀτιμάζων, "dishonoring"), capturing the contempt aspect.

— LOGOS
